policy_statement: Increase public education on clearing catch basins.
mechanism_description: Increase public education on clearing catch basins to help keep drainage systems functioning and reduce stormwater backups and flood risk.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heavy rainfall) → stormwater system blockage risk → ↑ urban flooding/exposure → public action keeps inlets clear → ↓ blockage → ↓ flood extent and impacts
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in human activity/system: Residents/businesses are informed and prompted to clear leaves/debris from catch basins/storm drains and to avoid dumping materials that clog inlets. (2) Mediating physical process: Reduced inlet obstruction increases stormwater capture and conveyance capacity, lowering ponding and surcharge/backups in local drainage networks. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced likelihood and severity of pluvial (rainfall-driven) flooding and associated disruption/damage under more frequent/intense precipitation extremes.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced flood exposure and damages during extreme rainfall by preventing stormwater drainage blockages.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism targets maintaining stormwater drainage performance to reduce flooding impacts from heavy rain—an exposure/vulnerability reduction pathway. 2) Why this primary over alternatives: Any mitigation/resource-efficiency effects (e.g., avoided cleanup energy use) are incidental and not the main causal chain; the intent and main effect is flood risk reduction. 3) Edge cases applied: Even if framed as “good housekeeping” or maintenance, the climate-relevant benefit is chiefly resilience to extreme precipitation; it does not inherently create carbon sinks or reduce direct emissions. 4) Applies to ALL instances: Public education on clearing catch basins consistently operates through keeping conveyance inlets open to reduce stormwater backups/flooding, which is adaptation. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., community preparedness vs. stormwater O&M) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a program is primarily aimed at reducing pollutant loads to waterways (water quality) rather than flood risk, the non-climate co-benefit could dominate; however, for this stated mechanism (blockage and flood risk reduction), Adaptation remains primary.
co_benefits: Reduced localized nuisance water/ponding; improved general stormwater system performance and cleanliness
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be paired with volunteer “adopt-a-drain” programs (which might look like an incentive/programmatic instrument), but the text as written specifies education only.
